questions.specification:
A start-up, **PulsePod**, is developing a refillable insulated bottle that tracks hydration and sends reminders through an app. The team is unsure which customer segment to prioritise and which features justify a higher price.

Before committing to tooling, PulsePod ran a **two‑stage market research plan**:

- **Stage 1 (exploratory):** 12 online interviews with runners, office workers and students.
- **Stage 2 (quantitative):** a survey of **240** potential customers recruited through social media ads.

The survey results are summarised below.

| Finding | Result |
|---|---:|
| Would consider buying a bottle in the next 3 months | 58% |
| Most important purchase driver | Leak-proof lid (34%), keeps drinks cold (29%), price (21%), app reminders (16%) |
| Most important purchase driver by segment | Runners: keeps drinks cold (38%), leak-proof lid (33%), price (17%), app reminders (12%); Office workers: leak-proof lid (39%), price (24%), keeps drinks cold (22%), app reminders (15%); Students: price (36%), leak-proof lid (28%), app reminders (20%), keeps drinks cold (16%) |
| Preferred buying channel | Online marketplace (44%), brand website (31%), sports store (25%) |
| Acceptable price range (most common) | $25$–$35$ |

PulsePod’s draft concept sheet (used in interviews and the survey) included:
- three colour options (black, mint, sand)
- two lid types (flip-top, straw)
- optional app subscription ($1.99/month$) for reminders and weekly reports

PulsePod’s investor asks for evidence that the product will meet real customer needs and that the marketing plan is based on reliable research.

![concept sheet showing a smart insulated bottle with two lid options, three colours, and an app subscription note](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/notes/ef08de98-496e-45c0-9bb6-d5412b72b474.png)

Figure 1: PulsePod draft concept sheet

![survey dashboard with bar charts for drivers, buying channel, and a price range band highlighted $25$–$35$](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/notes/1cf8a34f-2118-4852-8ac5-7c1f2055a1da.png)

Figure 2: Summary of PulsePod survey results

parts.content: State two purposes of market research for a company at PulsePod’s stage of product development.
parts.markscheme:
- Identifies/validates customer needs and preferences to reduce risk of developing the wrong product <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Estimates demand/market size or likelihood of purchase to support viability/forecasting <marks>1 mark</marks>

_**OR**_

- Informs pricing decisions (acceptable price range/willingness to pay) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Informs target market selection/segmentation and positioning <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Informs route-to-market/marketing mix (channels, promotion messages) <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Outline how PulsePod could use the Stage 2 survey findings to guide two design decisions for the product or service.
parts.markscheme:
- Prioritise design features aligned to key drivers (e.g., improve leak-proofing/lid sealing; enhance insulation performance) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Select lid type(s) or offer variants informed by stated preferences/importance of leak-proofing (e.g., refine flip-top or straw for leak resistance) <marks>1 mark</marks>

_**OR**_

- Adjust product/app offer because app reminders are lowest driver (e.g., make app optional, simplify, or bundle rather than lead with it) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Align specification/finishes to the price band $25$–$35$ by reducing/adding features to hit target cost-value balance <marks>1 mark</marks>

<note>Max 2 marks</note>

parts.content: Outline two pieces of design guidance (requirements) PulsePod should apply when creating the questionnaire for Stage 2 to improve the quality of the data.
parts.markscheme:
- Use clear, unambiguous wording and define terms (e.g., what “consider buying” means; timeframe) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Avoid leading/loaded questions; keep questions neutral <marks>1 mark</marks>

_**OR**_

- Use appropriate question types and response scales (e.g., consistent Likert scales; mutually exclusive options) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Pilot the questionnaire to identify misunderstandings/biased wording and improve reliability <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Ensure logical order/flow and limit length to reduce respondent fatigue <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Include demographic/segment questions needed for analysis (e.g., age, activity type) while complying with ethics/privacy <marks>1 mark</marks>

<note>Max 2 marks</note>

parts.content: Explain how PulsePod could apply two different market research strategies to reduce uncertainty before investing in tooling and launch.
parts.markscheme:
- Use focus groups or additional interviews with defined segments to explore motivations, barriers, and language for positioning (qualitative insight to refine concept) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Use a concept test/A-B test of feature bundles (e.g., with/without subscription; lid types) to quantify preference trade-offs <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Use prototype/user testing (observational research) to verify usability and validate leak-proof/insulation claims in real contexts <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Use competitor/market desk research to benchmark price points, claims, and differentiation and identify gaps <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Use a small-scale pilot launch or crowdfunding pre-orders to validate willingness to pay and demand with real purchase behaviour <marks>1 mark</marks>

<note>Max 5 marks</note>

parts.content: Discuss how PulsePod should interpret and use its market research to decide (i) a primary target segment and (ii) an initial marketing mix (product, price, place, promotion).
parts.markscheme:
- Segments the data (e.g., runners vs office workers vs students) and selects a primary segment based on strongest need-fit and purchase intent <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Justifies segment choice using evidence (e.g., segment prioritises leak-proof/insulation; aligns with app value; higher willingness to pay) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Product: prioritises features supported by findings (leak-proofing, cold retention) and positions app as supporting feature or optional add-on <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Price: sets initial price within/around $25$–$35$ and explains how this supports adoption and margin; considers tiering/bundles <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Place: focuses on online marketplace and brand website as primary channels, reflecting stated preferences; considers sports retail selectively <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Promotion: develops messages around top drivers (leak-proof, cold) and uses language learned from qualitative research; selects channels aligned to segment (e.g., social ads/influencers if runners) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Evaluates limitations of the research (sampling via social ads, self-report bias, sample size, representativeness) and states how to mitigate (triangulation, further research) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Explains how insights will be fed back into iterative design decisions and success metrics (e.g., conversion rate, return rate, subscription uptake) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Makes a balanced judgement, referencing evidence and acknowledging uncertainty <marks>1 mark</marks>

<note>Max 9 marks</note>

questions.specification:
A start-up called **PulseWear** is planning a new line of smartwatches. PulseWear is deciding which **market sector(s)** to enter and how to **segment** potential customers so the product can be designed to match their needs.

PulseWear’s research summary is below.

**Products being considered**
- **Model A – Active**: rugged case, water resistance, long battery life, GPS, limited colour options, basic packaging.
- **Model B – Studio**: slimmer profile, premium finishes, interchangeable straps, smaller battery, more colourways, gift-style packaging.

**Survey highlights**
- Group 1: Ages $16$–$24$, tight budgets, influenced by trends and colour choice, upgrades frequently.
- Group 2: Ages $25$–$45$, health-focused, values reliability and battery life, wants clear information at purchase.
- Group 3: Ages $35$–$60$, higher disposable income, prefers premium look/feel and gifting, expects strong after-sales support.

PulseWear plans to sell through an online configurator where customers can choose the model, colour, strap type and optional accessories before checkout.

![A webpage mock-up for the PulseWear online configurator showing steps 'Choose model', 'Pick colours', 'Select strap', 'Add-ons', and 'Checkout', with a running total price displayed on the right.](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/notes/eb610501-0a27-478e-9b88-6508e15df0c4.png)

![A checkout summary mock-up listing the base watch price plus selected strap and add-ons, showing a final total and delivery estimate.](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/notes/25b7b669-d043-43cc-ad51-f4f3a45ade6b.png)

parts.content: a) State two different market sectors that PulseWear could be operating in with a smartwatch product.  [2]
parts.markscheme:
- Identifies an appropriate market sector such as consumer electronics/wearables. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Identifies a second appropriate market sector such as health/fitness, sports/leisure, fashion/accessories, or gifting/luxury goods. <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Outline how segmenting customers could help PulseWear decide what features to include in Model A and Model B.
parts.markscheme:
- Links segmentation to matching features to the needs/wants of a specific group (eg battery life/ruggedness for health/active users; premium finishes/colourways for style/gifting users). <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Explains that segmentation reduces wasted features/costs by prioritising what each segment values, improving perceived suitability. <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Outline one suitable way PulseWear could segment the smartwatch market using the survey information.
parts.markscheme:
- Uses a valid segmentation basis (eg demographic by age bands, psychographic by lifestyle/values, behavioural by upgrade frequency or gifting). <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Applies it to the data by identifying a segment (eg “trend-driven $16$–$24$” or “reliability-focused health users $25$–$45$”). <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Explain how design guidance for market sectors should influence PulseWear’s design decisions for its chosen target segment(s).
parts.markscheme:
- Chooses a sector/segment and derives design requirements from it (eg sports/health sector implies durability, water resistance, readable UI, battery life). <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Explains how aesthetics/CMF (colour, material, finish) and form factor should align with the segment (eg premium finishes and slimmer profile for fashion/gifting). <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Explains how usability and information at point of purchase should align with sector expectations (eg clear specifications, sizing/strap guidance, accessibility). <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Explains how packaging/after-sales/support or sustainability expectations can vary by sector and should be designed accordingly (eg gift packaging and strong support for premium buyers; minimal packaging for budget segment). <marks>1 mark</marks>

questions.specification:
**EcoHome**

A company, EcoHome, specializes in producing eco-friendly home appliances. They are planning to launch a new product line aimed at energy-efficient kitchen appliances. 

The company wants to understand the market sectors and segments to target their new products effectively. The following diagram shows the different market sectors and segments for kitchen appliances based on factors such as income level, age group, and lifestyle preferences. 

![A diagram showing a pie chart of market sectors and segments for kitchen appliances. The chart is divided into three main sectors: Low-income, Middle-income, and High-income. Each sector is further divided into segments based on age groups: Youth (18-35), Adults (36-55), and Seniors (56+). Additionally, each age group segment is subdivided by lifestyle preferences: Eco-conscious, Tech-savvy, and Traditional.](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/generated/d0eac514-8ca6-4d3b-b03a-41baf741fef2.png)

Using this context, answer the following questions:

parts.content: Evaluate the challenges EcoHome might face when targeting multiple market segments simultaneously for their new product line.
parts.markscheme:
- Resource allocation challenges: dividing budget, time, and personnel across segments can strain company capabilities and affect marketing quality <marks>1 mark</marks>

- Brand consistency issues: difficulty maintaining consistent brand message/image while targeting diverse segments with different needs and preferences <marks>1 mark</marks>

- Product differentiation complexity: challenges in developing products that cater to specific segment requirements without overlap or market confusion <marks>1 mark</marks>

- Market research demands: significant effort and investment needed to understand unique characteristics of each segment and adapt strategies accordingly <marks>1 mark</marks>

<note>Maximum 4 marks, award 1 mark for each distinct challenge evaluated</note>

parts.content: Identify and describe the three main market sectors shown in the diagram for kitchen appliances.
parts.markscheme:
- Low-income sector identified <marks>1 mark</marks>

- Description: Price-sensitive consumers seeking affordable appliances <marks>1 mark</marks>

- Middle-income sector identified <marks>1 mark</marks>

- Description: Consumers balancing cost and quality in purchases <marks>1 mark</marks>

- High-income sector identified <marks>1 mark</marks>

- Description: Consumers willing to pay premium prices for high-quality, feature-rich appliances <marks>1 mark</marks>

<note>Maximum 2 marks per sector (1 for identification + 1 for description)</note>

parts.content: Explain how EcoHome can use market segmentation to target their new energy-efficient kitchen appliances to different age groups within the middle-income sector.
parts.markscheme:
- Identification and explanation for Youth (18-35) segment: <marks>2 marks</marks>
  - Values modern, stylish appliances with smart features
  - Marketing focus on advanced technology and sleek designs

- Identification and explanation for Adults (36-55) segment: <marks>2 marks</marks>
  - Prioritizes functionality and reliability
  - Marketing focus on energy savings and durability

- Identification and explanation for Seniors (56+) segment: <marks>2 marks</marks>
  - Looks for easy-to-use and accessible appliances
  - Marketing focus on simplicity and user-friendliness

<note>Award 1 mark for identifying each age group and 1 mark for appropriate targeting explanation, up to 2 marks maximum per group</note>

parts.content: Discuss the potential benefits for EcoHome in targeting the eco-conscious lifestyle segment across all income levels.
parts.markscheme:
- Increased Market Reach: By targeting eco-conscious consumers across low, middle, and high-income levels, EcoHome can expand its customer base and reach a wider audience <marks>1 mark</marks>

- Brand Loyalty: Eco-conscious consumers tend to be loyal to brands that align with their values, allowing EcoHome to build strong brand loyalty and repeat business <marks>1 mark</marks>

- Positive Brand Image: Targeting the eco-conscious segment enhances EcoHome's reputation as a responsible and environmentally-friendly company, attracting positive attention and potential partnerships <marks>1 mark</marks>

- Competitive Advantage: Growing demand for eco-friendly products allows EcoHome to differentiate itself by offering energy-efficient kitchen appliances that meet eco-conscious consumer needs <marks>1 mark</marks>

<note>Award 1 mark for each distinct benefit discussed up to maximum 4 marks</note>

parts.content: Describe how lifestyle preferences influence the design and features of kitchen appliances in the high-income sector.
parts.markscheme:
- Eco-conscious consumers influence design through: <marks>1 mark</marks>
  - Energy-efficient features
  - Sustainable materials
  - Water-saving modes
  - Recyclable components <marks>1 mark</marks>

- Tech-savvy consumers influence design through: <marks>1 mark</marks>
  - Smart connectivity features
  - Touch-screen controls
  - Integration with smart home devices <marks>1 mark</marks>

- Traditional consumers influence design through: <marks>1 mark</marks>
  - Classic, timeless designs
  - High-quality craftsmanship
  - Focus on reliable performance over advanced features <marks>1 mark</marks>

<note>Maximum 2 marks for each lifestyle preference (1 mark for identifying + 1 mark for describing influences)</note>

questions.specification:
A start-up, Hush & Halo, is developing a rechargeable bedside lamp with an app that can automate lighting routines. The company is deciding which market sector(s) to enter first and how to segment customers.

Hush & Halo has produced two early concepts and a short set of research notes.

**Concept A: “Halo Home”**
- Brushed aluminium base, warm white dimmable light, fabric shade
- One physical dial; no account required
- £39 target retail price
- Packaging: minimal, designed for shelf display in a homeware store

**Concept B: “Halo Pro”**
- Polycarbonate body, RGB + tunable white, voice-assistant compatible
- App features: scheduling, multiple-user control, firmware updates
- £89 target retail price
- Packaging: includes QR set-up guide; designed for online unboxing and returns

**Research notes (extract)**
- Retailers approached: a DIY/home-improvement chain, a homeware boutique group, and an office supplies distributor.
- Survey (n=120):
  - 44% want “simple, no app”
  - 38% want “smart features and automation”
  - 18% are “price-first”
- Interviews suggest three recurring customer types:
  - Students in shared housing who move frequently
  - Young families concerned about bedtime routines
  - Small office managers buying in multiples

![Two alternative bedside lamp concept sketches on a single page: left sketch shows a traditional fabric-shade lamp with a single control dial on the base; right sketch shows a modern cylindrical lamp with a translucent body and a small indicator light, annotated with app/voice-control features.](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/notes/a08c5be2-dce0-4100-a795-bca3fc2e3e3e.png)

Figure 1: Early concept sketches for “Halo Home” and “Halo Pro”

parts.content: Identify two market sectors that are plausible routes to market for Hush & Halo’s lamp (e.g., consumer, commercial, education, etc.).
parts.markscheme:
- Correctly identifies a plausible sector for the product (e.g., consumer/household, commercial/office, education/student accommodation, hospitality) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Correctly identifies a second plausible sector <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Outline how segmenting the market could help Hush & Halo decide between Concept A and Concept B.
parts.markscheme:
- Shows how segmentation clarifies different customer needs/preferences (e.g., simplicity vs smart automation) to match a concept <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Links segmentation to a business/design decision (e.g., prioritising a segment, positioning, channel choice, pricing) <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Using the case study, state two appropriate segmentation variables Hush & Halo could use to define target customer groups.
parts.markscheme:
- States an appropriate segmentation variable (e.g., demographic such as age/life stage; geographic; psychographic such as attitudes to tech; behavioural such as usage/benefits sought; purchasing role/B2B volume) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- States a second appropriate segmentation variable <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Explain how choosing a specific market sector could influence design guidance for the lamp’s packaging and set-up information.
parts.markscheme:
- Explains a sector-specific packaging requirement (e.g., retail shelf presence/theft prevention/compliance vs e-commerce protection/returns) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Explains a sector-specific set-up/onboarding requirement (e.g., simple printed instructions for consumer; QR/IT policy guidance for offices) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Links guidance to user context (e.g., shared housing, families, office procurement) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Considers constraints/standards typical of sectors (e.g., safety labelling, warranty info, accessibility, multilingual needs) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Makes a justified comparison between at least two sectors or channels (e.g., homeware retail vs office distributor vs online) <marks>1 mark</marks>

<note>Max 5 marks</note>

parts.content: e) Discuss how Hush & Halo should adapt the lamp’s features and marketing message for three different market segments identified or implied in the case study.  [9]
parts.markscheme:
- Segment 1: clearly describes the segment and adapts features and message appropriately (e.g., students: portability, durability, low cost, easy set-up; message focused on moving/space-saving) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Provides a justified design feature choice for segment 1 (e.g., compact, replaceable shade, universal charger) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Segment 2: clearly describes the segment and adapts features and message appropriately (e.g., young families: warm dimming, routines, night mode; message focused on bedtime consistency/safety) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Provides a justified design feature choice for segment 2 (e.g., child-safe controls, preset routines, low-glare shade) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Segment 3: clearly describes the segment and adapts features and message appropriately (e.g., small offices: bulk purchase, admin control, standardisation; message focused on productivity/procurement value) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Provides a justified design feature choice for segment 3 (e.g., central settings, robust materials, replaceable parts, asset tagging) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Shows understanding of sector/segment fit (B2C vs B2B expectations, buying behaviour, distribution channel) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Considers pricing/positioning trade-offs between Concept A and B for segments (e.g., £39 simplicity vs £89 smart premium) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Coherent conclusion recommending which concept(s) to prioritise for launch with justification based on segmentation and sector guidance <marks>1 mark</marks>

<note>Max 9 marks</note>
